1489	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1490	
1491	\begin{lemma}
1492	
1493	\leanok
1494	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1495	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1497	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1498	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
1499	  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
1500	  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
1501	  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	\begin{proof}
1504	
1505	\leanok
1506	  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
1507	  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
1508	  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
1509	  the claimed equality.
1510	\end{proof}
1511	
1512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1513	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1514	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1516	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1517	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1518	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype,
1519	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1520	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1521	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1522	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1523	  %   intersection dimension).
1524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1525	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1526	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1527	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1528	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1529	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1530	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1531	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1532	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1533	  \[
1534	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1535	  \]
1536	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1537	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1538	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1539	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1540	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1541	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1543	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1544	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1545	\end{lemma}
1546	
1547	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1548	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1549	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1550	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1551	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1552	%  following short exact sequence
1553	%  $$
1554	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1555	%  $$
1556	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1557	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1558	\begin{proof}
1559	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1560	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1561	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1562	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1563	    \leanok
1564	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1565	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1566	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1567	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1568	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1569	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1570	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1571	
1572	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1573	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1574	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1575	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1576	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1577	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1578	
1579	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1580	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1581	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1582	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1583	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1584	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1585	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1586	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1587	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1588	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1589	
1590	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1591	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1592	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1593	  \[
1594	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1595	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1596	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1597	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1598	  \]
1599	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1600	\end{proof}

1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).

1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}

1653	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and

1730	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.
1738	
1739	\begin{definition}
1740	
1741	\leanok
1742	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1743	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}

1771	\begin{lemma}
1772	
1773	\leanok
1774	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1775	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1777	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1778	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1779	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1780	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1781	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1782	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1783	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1784	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1785	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1786	\end{lemma}

1788	\begin{lemma}
1789	
1790	\leanok
1791	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1792	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1794	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1795	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1796	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1797	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1798	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1799	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1800	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1801	  \[
1802	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1803	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1804	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1805	      \;\in\; P' .
1806	  \]
1807	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1808	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1809	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1810	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1811	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1812	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1813	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1814	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1815	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1816	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1817	\end{lemma}

1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure

1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).

1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.

1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators

1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1919	  \[
1920	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection

1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available

1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1950	
1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}
1968	
1969	\begin{lemma}
1970	
1971	\leanok
1972	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1976	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1978	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1980	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1981	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1982	\end{lemma}

1984	\begin{definition}
1985	
1986	\leanok
1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1991	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1992	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2007	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.
2013	\end{definition}

2017	\leanok
2018	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2019	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2021	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2022	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2023	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2024	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2025	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2026	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2027	  length-\(r\) datum
2028	  \[
2029	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2030	  \]
2031	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2032	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2033	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2034	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2035	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2036	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2037	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then

2040	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2041	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2042	\end{definition}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2099	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2100	
2101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2102	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2103	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2106	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2107	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2108	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2109	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2110	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2115	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2116	\end{lemma}
2117	
2118	\begin{lemma}
2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2234	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2235	
2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2330	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2331	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2332	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2333	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2334	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2336	  % Polynomials" (read from
2337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2339	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2340	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2341	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2342	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2343	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2344	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2345	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2346	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2347	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2348	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2349	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2350	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2351	  \[
2352	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2353	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2354	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2355	  \]
2356	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2357	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2358	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2359	
2360	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2361	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2362	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2363	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2364	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2365	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2366	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2367	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2368	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2369	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2370	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2371	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2372	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2373	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2374	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2375	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2376	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2377	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2378	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2379	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2380	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2381	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2382	
2383	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2384	\end{definition}
2385	
2386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2387	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2388	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2390	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2391	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2392	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2393	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2394	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2395	  \(U\):
2396	  \[
2397	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2398	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2399	  \]
2400	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2401	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2402	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2403	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2404	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2405	\end{lemma}
2406	
2407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2408	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2409	  localization]
2410	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2411	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2412	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2413	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2414	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2415	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2416	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2417	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2418	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2419	  \[
2420	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2421	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2422	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2423	  \]
2424	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2425	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2426	
2427	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2428	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2429	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2430	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2431	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2432	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2433	\end{lemma}
2434	
2435	\begin{proof}
2436	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2437	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2438	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2439	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2440	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2441	
2442	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2443	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2444	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2445	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2446	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2447	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2448	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2449	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2450	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2451	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2452	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2453	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2454	\end{proof}
2455	
2456	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2457	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2458	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2459	  \mathlibok
2460	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2461	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2462	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2463	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2464	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2465	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2466	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2467	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2468	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2469	\end{lemma}

2472	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}

2475	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}

2480	  %             sheaf on Spec R, built directly from QuasicoherentData — NO IsIso assumption)
2481	  %   → gap1   (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}: IsIso M.fromTildeΓ, via the stalk/section
2482	  %             assembly from G1-core)

2484	  % The affine engine `isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ` (built, takes [IsIso]) is a
2485	  % DOWNSTREAM convenience once gap1 holds, NOT a prerequisite of gap1. G1-core is the genuine
2486	  % foundational gap and the mathlib-build target; it discharges the affine instance of this keystone
2487	  % directly (X = Spec Γ(X,U), U = ⊤) and is reused by gap2.
2488	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2489	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2490	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2491	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2492	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2493	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2494	  %  associated to $M$.
2495	  %  [...]
2496	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2497	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2498	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2499	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2500	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2501	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2502	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2503	  \begin{enumerate}
2504	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2505	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2506	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2507	    \item the section-restriction map
2508	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2509	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2510	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2511	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2512	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2513	  \end{enumerate}
2514	\end{lemma}
2515	
2516	\begin{proof}
2517	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2518	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2519	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2520	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2521	
2522	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2523	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2524	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2525	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2526	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2527	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2528	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2529	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2530	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2531	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2532	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2533	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2534	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2535	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2536	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2537	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2538	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2539	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2540	\end{proof}

2550	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, part (2).}
2551	  Let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module and \(f \in R\). Then the presheaf restriction map of the
2552	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) on \(\Spec R\) from the global sections
2553	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) to the sections on the basic open \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\)
2554	  exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) as the localized module
2555	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\); that is, it is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\)

2558	\begin{proof}

2560	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the map
2561	  \((\mathrm{tilde.toOpen}\,N\,(D f))\) (the canonical map \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\)).
2562	  Since \(\mathrm{tilde.toOpen}\,N\,\top\) is an isomorphism and
2563	  \(\mathrm{tilde.toOpen}\,N\,(D f) = \mathrm{tilde.toOpen}\,N\,\top \,\circ\, \mathrm{restriction}\),
2564	  precomposing the localization map with the inverse isomorphism transfers the

2592	\begin{lemma}[Section-localization for a quasi-coherent sheaf on $\Spec R$ (G1-core)]
2593	  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}
2594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_isQuasicoherent}
2595	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2596	  % NOTE: the genuine Mathlib-absent foundational gap; the Lean decl does NOT yet exist. This is the
2597	  % affine instance (X = Spec R, U = ⊤) of \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, proved
2598	  % DIRECTLY from QuasicoherentData (no IsIso/Presentation assumption), and is what gap1 and the
2599	  % general keystone both reduce to. Build target for the QUOT mathlib-build lane (route in
2600	  % analogies/quot-qcoh-affine-globalization.md, Route C / Stacks 01HA).
2601	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", tag 01HA (\S Quasi-coherent sheaves,
2602	  % "$\mathcal{F}(D(f)) = \mathcal{F}(X)_f$ for $\mathcal{F}$ quasi-coherent on affine $X$")
2603	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex / stacks-properties.tex). Cf. [Hartshorne], II.5.2.
2604	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01HA; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.5.2.}
2605	  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\). Then the section
2606	  restriction map \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) as the
2607	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top)\); i.e.\ it is
2608	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).
2609	\end{lemma}
2610	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: the descent is the standard cover-by-basic-opens + flat-localization argument;
2611	% transcribed in project notation below (Stacks 01HA proof; references/stacks-properties.tex).
2612	\begin{proof}
2613	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2614	  Write \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). The map is built and verified in four steps (the cover-refine →
2615	  local-tilde → flat-equalizer descent):
2616	  \begin{enumerate}
2617	    \item \emph{Refine the quasi-coherence cover to basic opens.} The quasi-coherence datum gives an
2618	      open cover of \(\Spec R\) on each member of which \(M\) is a cokernel of free module sheaves.
2619	      Basic opens \(D(g)\) form a basis of \(\Spec R\), so refine to a cover by basic opens; since
2620	      \(\Spec R\) is quasi-compact, finitely many \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) suffice, and the \(g_a\)
2621	      generate the unit ideal.
2622	    \item \emph{Locally \(M\) is a $\widetilde{(-)}$ sheaf.} On each \(D(g_a) \cong \Spec R_{g_a}\)
2623	      the local cokernel-of-frees presentation makes \(M|_{D(g_a)} \cong \widetilde{N_a}\) for the
2624	      \(R_{g_a}\)-module \(N_a = \Gamma(M, D(g_a))\), using that \(\Gamma(\Spec R, D(g_a)) = R_{g_a}\)
2625	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
2626	    \item \emph{Sheaf equalizer + flat descent.} The sheaf condition over the finite cover
2627	      \(\{D(g_a)\}\) presents \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\) as the equalizer
2628	      \(N \to \prod_a N_a \rightrightarrows \prod_{a,b} \Gamma(M, D(g_a g_b))\). Localizing this
2629	      finite equalizer at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) is exact (localization is flat, hence preserves
2630	      finite limits), and the localized diagram is the same equalizer computing \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\)
2631	      over the cover \(\{D(g_a) \cap D(f)\}\). Hence \(N_f \cong \Gamma(M, D(f))\), compatibly with
2632	      the restriction map.
2633	    \item Therefore \(\Gamma(M,\top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is the localization \(N \to N_f\), i.e.\
2634	      \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\).
2635	  \end{enumerate}
2636	  The flat-localization-of-a-finite-equalizer step is the substantive content; everything else is
2637	  the affine dictionary already available.
2638	\end{proof}

2640	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent on an affine has trivial $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1)]
2641	  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2642	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent}
2643	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}

2648	  % and `FullyFaithful.reflectsIsomorphisms` (via the project's SpecModulesToSheafFullyFaithful).

2650	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
2651	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1279--1284).
2652	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
2653	  % be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
2654	  % $\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the $R$-module $\Gamma(X,
2655	  % \mathcal{F})$."
2656	  \textit{The affine equivalence QCoh\((\Spec R)\) \(\simeq\) Mod\((R)\): a quasi-coherent module
2657	  sheaf on \(\Spec R\) is \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M)}\) up to canonical isomorphism.}
2658	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) that is quasi-coherent
2659	  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism;
2660	  equivalently \(M\) lies in the essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \to (\Spec
2661	  R).\mathrm{Modules}\).
2662	\end{lemma}
2663	\begin{proof}
2664	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2665	  Set \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). The counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde N \to M\) is an
2666	  isomorphism iff its image under the fully faithful \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) is, and the
2667	  latter is a morphism of sheaves of \(R\)-modules on \(\Spec R\), so it suffices to check it is an
2668	  isomorphism on each basic open \(D(f)\) (basic opens form a basis; a sheaf morphism that is an
2669	  isomorphism on a basis of opens is an isomorphism). On \(D(f)\) the component is
2670	  \(N_f \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f)) = N_f\) localizes \(N\) at
2671	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) by \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}, and the target
2672	  \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) localizes \(N\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) by
2673	  \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization} (G1-core); the map between them is the canonical one
2674	  (compatibility \(\mathrm{toOpen\_fromTildeΓ\_app}\)). Two localizations of \(N\) at the same
2675	  submonoid intertwined by the localization maps are uniquely isomorphic
2676	  (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv}\)), so the component is an isomorphism. Hence
2677	  \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism.
2678	\end{proof}

2714	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2715	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2716	  \[

2718	  \]

2720	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
2721	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
2722	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2723	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2724	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.

2727	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2728	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2729	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2730	  \mathlibok
2731	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2732	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2733	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2734	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2735	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2736	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2737	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2738	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2739	\end{lemma}

2876	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
2877	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
2878	
2879	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
2880	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
2881	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
2882	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

3002	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
3003	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
3004	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
3005	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
3006	  Explained Ch.~5).}
3007	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
3008	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
3009	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
3010	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
3011	  equipped with a tautological quotient
3012	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
3013	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
3014	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
3015	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
3016	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
3017	
3018	\end{theorem}
3019	
3020	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
3021	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
3022	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
3023	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
3024	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
3025	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
3026	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
3027	\begin{proof}
3028	  
3029	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
3030	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
3031	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
3032	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
3033	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
3034	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
3035	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
3036	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
3037	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
3038	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
3039	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
3040	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
3041	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
3042	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
3043	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
3044	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
3045	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
3046	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
3047	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
3048	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
3049	
3050	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
3051	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
3052	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
3053	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
3054	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
3055	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
3056	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
3057	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
3058	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
3059	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
3060	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
3061	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
3062	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
3063	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
3064	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
3065	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
3066	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
3067	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
3068	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
3069	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
3070	\end{proof}
